title: Counsel and Might — The Blueprint and the Build
reading:
God rarely releases power without first giving direction, and He rarely gives direction without supplying the power to carry it out. Counsel and Might are paired in Isaiah 11 for this reason: one without the other is incomplete.

Counsel is the blueprint. Might is the build.

Moses is one of Scripture's clearest examples of both operating together. At the burning bush, God gave him specific counsel: go to Pharaoh, lead Israel out of Egypt, speak these words. Every instruction was precise. But the instructions alone would have produced nothing. What followed was Might — signs, wonders, ten plagues, the parting of the Red Sea. The Counsel told Moses where to go and what to do. The Might made it possible.

Joshua's battle at Jericho operates on the same principle. The divine counsel was unconventional to the point of appearing foolish: march around the walls for seven days, blow trumpets, and shout. There was no military logic behind it. But the Counsel was specific, and when it was obeyed, the Might fell — the walls collapsed. Counsel came first. Might followed obedience.

Gideon's story pushes this further. God reduced his army from 32,000 to 300 before sending them into battle. The Counsel appeared to guarantee defeat. But God's instruction was also God's promise — and the Might that followed turned the enemy against itself without Gideon's army lifting a single weapon. The strategy was given to make it unmistakable that the victory was God's.

Jehoshaphat faces three armies simultaneously with no military answer. The divine Counsel arrives: "You will not need to fight in this battle. Position yourselves, stand still and see the salvation of the LORD" (2 Chronicles 20:17). The Might that followed destroyed the enemy armies completely while Israel worshipped. The Counsel was the key — not because it explained the mechanism, but because following it positioned them to receive the Might.

The pattern is consistent: God speaks (Counsel), the people obey (faith), and God acts (Might). Counsel without Might produces good intentions with no power. Might without Counsel produces power without direction. Together they form God's pattern of strategy followed by supernatural accomplishment.
reflection:
Is there an area of your life where you sense God has given you clear direction but you are waiting for the strength to follow through — or where you have been acting in your own strength without seeking His direction first?

What does it look like practically to wait for Counsel before you move, and to trust that the Might will follow?

How does Jehoshaphat's story challenge your assumptions about what spiritual warfare or a difficult problem should look like?
abide: Lord, I want to move when You say move and stand when You say stand. Teach me to seek Your Counsel before I act and to trust that when You give direction, You supply everything needed to carry it out. Where I have been running ahead of You in my own strength, slow me down. Where I have been waiting when You have already spoken, give me the courage to step out. Let Counsel and Might work together in my life the way they did in Scripture.
